tables.title: Cardiac Arrest Drug Reference — Doses, Indications, and Key Facts
rows: 300 mg first dose; 150 mg second dose
Preferred antiarrhythmic; dilute in NS (NOT D5W); Class I/III/IV properties
Preferred in acute ischemia/MI; Class Ib antiarrhythmic
Hyperkalemia, TCA OD, bicarbonate-responsive acidosis only
NOT routine; risk of hypernatremia and paradoxical acidosis
NOT for ordinary VF/pVT; stabilizes membrane in prolonged QT
No advantage over epi alone; epinephrine preferred
NEVER in pulseless arrest; 6-sec half-life; needs pulse to reach heart

tables.title: IV vs IO Access During Cardiac Arrest
rows: Reasonable when IV not quickly obtainable
Dependent on patient factors (veins, edema, arrest)
Fast — can be placed in most locations within 1–2 min
Comparable to IV — drugs reach central circulation
Difficult IV access, obese patients, cardiac arrest

tables.title: Drugs NOT Indicated in Cardiac Arrest
rows: Half-life of 6 seconds; requires pulse to reach heart; no effect on VF/pVT/asystole/PEA
Regular narrow-complex SVT with a pulse
No longer recommended for cardiac arrest (removed from ACLS algorithms)
Symptomatic bradycardia with a pulse (1 mg IV q3–5 min, max 3 mg)
Anaphylaxis (0.3–0.5 mg IM); use 1:10,000 for arrest
No routine role in cardiac arrest
Hyperkalemia, calcium channel blocker OD, hypermagnesemia
